front: What is Timestamp Ordering (TSO) in concurrency control?
back: A concurrency control protocol that uses transaction timestamps to determine the serializability order. Transactions are ordered based on their start times, and operations are accepted only if they do not violate timestamp order.

front: What is a timestamp in database concurrency control?
back: A unique identifier assigned to each transaction when it begins, typically using system clock or a logical counter. It represents the transaction's start time and determines its priority in the execution order.

front: Define Read Timestamp (RTS) of a data item
back: The largest timestamp among all transactions that have successfully read the data item. It records the most recent transaction that read the data item.

front: Define Write Timestamp (WTS) of a data item
back: The largest timestamp among all transactions that have successfully written the data item. It records the most recent transaction that wrote the data item.

front: What is the read rule in Timestamp Ordering protocol?
back: A transaction T with timestamp TS(T) can read a data item Q only if TS(T) >= Write_Timestamp(WTS(Q)). Otherwise, the read is rejected and T is rolled back.

front: What is the write rule in Timestamp Ordering protocol?
back: A transaction T with timestamp TS(T) can write a data item Q only if TS(T) >= Read_Timestamp(RTS(Q)) AND TS(T) >= Write_Timestamp(WTS(Q)). Otherwise, the write is rejected and T is rolled back.

front: What is Thomas' Write Rule in timestamp ordering?
back: A modification to basic TO that allows certain outdated writes to be ignored rather than causing rollback. If TS(T) < WTS(Q), the write is ignored because a newer value already exists. If TS(T) < RTS(Q), the write is rejected and T is rolled back.
A problem where the rollback of one transaction necessitates the rollback of other dependent transactions that have read data written by the rolled-back transaction, leading to a chain of rollbacks.

front: Does Timestamp Ordering cause cascading rollbacks?
back: No, one of the key advantages of Timestamp Ordering is that it does not cause cascading rollbacks. Each transaction executes independently based on its timestamp, so rollback of one transaction does not affect others.

front: What is the main disadvantage of Timestamp Ordering?
back: It may cause starvation for long-running transactions if younger transactions continuously keep updating data items that the older transaction needs to read or write.

front: What is the difference between basic TO and Thomas' Write Rule?
back: Basic TO rejects any write if TS(T) < WTS(Q) or TS(T) < RTS(Q). Thomas' Write Rule ignores writes with TS(T) < WTS(Q) and rejects only if TS(T) < RTS(Q), allowing more operations to proceed.
A variant where transactions are not allowed to read or write uncommitted data. It maintains a stricter order and may use a commit bit or delay writes until the transaction commits.

front: What is the purpose of validation phase in optimistic concurrency control?
back: To check if the transaction's operations would violate serializability before committing. It compares the transaction's timestamp with those of other concurrent transactions.

front: How does lock-based concurrency control differ from timestamp ordering?
back: Lock-based control uses locks to prevent conflicts (pessimistic approach), while timestamp ordering uses timestamps to order operations (optimistic approach). Locks block transactions; timestamps may cause rollback.

front: What happens when a transaction is rolled back in TO?
back: The transaction is assigned a new timestamp and restarted from the beginning. This ensures it gets a chance to execute with the latest data.
